item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good lord I want to paint this but the colors don't exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Technobabble requires a very specific finesse to pull off — the ability to juggle complex concepts, to reduce them to terms whose meaning can still be inferred or quickly explained, and to apply them in ways which are understandable in the context of the story. Am I going to claim I know how to achieve this perfectly? No. I've fucked up plenty of times, and for as long as I'm practicing I'll be fucking up in different ways.

However, there still comes a point when I'm reading something where, if it becomes so dense I lose track of what it is I'm supposed to be focusing on, I am going to be completely lost in the story. Not because I'm necessarily confused — I was able to follow along with the meaning here — but because I'm lost on what it is I'm supposed to care about. The technobabble here is layered so thick and so without relent that I don't know why I should care about any of what's happening. I tried to search for it, but by the halfway point I lost my patience.

I get that the intent here is for the mystery to be in deciphering what the terminology all means. That isn't something I inherently dislike. SCP-2719, one of my favorite articles, has an understanding which hinges on five words of technobabble. However, the difference between this and 2719 is that I could read 2719 without knowing the meaning and still be viscerally horrified. The surreal, apparent logic and the small details instill a reaction of "what the fuck" which is independent of knowing what the technobabble means. Said reaction is what also made me incessantly search for any explanation of what it is I had read. Not because of the terminology on its own, but because of everything else it supplemented — everything which made me care about what the terminology meant.

This article is the technobabble and the technobabble alone. I couldn't find a single reason for me to decipher a meaning through all of it.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This needs, like, a bazillion more footnotes explaining your core concepts, because I was lost from pretty much the word go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the reply! Yeah, I think that a lot of my confusion stemmed from not knowing about how the Noosphere worked exactly. Even with all the conceptual mindscrews that I've seen on this site, the Noosphere always manages to trip me up and freak me out. The fact that this article managed to perfectly capture that unease you feel from a bad dream, even outside of the logs, is a testament to your guys' skill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! We're actually currently trying to get more images for the logs, but finding semi-realistic surreal imagery under CC is surprisingly hard. By the end of the contest, we should have about one or two more images for the logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey! Thanks for the feedback, and I'm happy you enjoyed the article. I'm happy to adress some of this as best I can, but as you said yourself, this is a bit hard to describe in words. I'll try to go through line by line.

Collapsible for convenience
:)

The reason the anomaly is Safe is us really trying to lean into it as a containment class and a containment class only. We tried to allude to it in the article a bit, but it's not that just any artificial consciousness could stumble into the anomaly. It's things that are wrong or unhinged in some way. MARDUK.AIC was infected with a memetic hazard that it got from Foundation duties, the unknown was a pattern screamer (big reveal), and the third was made of a CotBG hive mind. Also it would require that consciousness to be powerful enough to enter the Noosphere in the first place. If you can't chuck yourself into the Noosphere using anomalous science, this thing can't really effect you.

I definitely understand confusion regarding the first sentence, because it was literally the first sentence that was written of this. "Artistic constructohazard" is a fancy way of describing what the anomaly does. It takes artistic concepts and uses them in physical constructions. It's biologically contained in the Noosphere because only unhinged technology can access it without assistance. Yes, a person could just physically enter the Noosphere through a Monolith, but that would first require a Monolith to be constructed and accessible. The three Monoliths in the log through are the only ones that were able to reach the Monolith stage, and its only because they were fast enough.

As for the confusion regarding what is affected, I think this is the best way to put it. The concepts that are mainly affected are ones that "make up" the person and their idea of creativity. 5000 wouldn't be able to spread to you because you aren't in the Noosphere.

Yes, this thing could cause some serious damage. But for the efforts of containment, there's not much you need to do. It's not like everyday people can shoot their consciousness into the Noosphere.

Hope this helped clear up things in one way or another!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot of good here, and I overall upvoted, but I feel the article could still be stronger.

The primary wide image was excellent as a hook and setting the scene, and the logs would've benefitted from more similarly-edited fever-dream images throughout the logs.

Secondly, the log gets a bit dense at times and can be hard to parse. Breaking up the visual profile with images would help, as would simplifying or trimming.

Overall nice work, enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, but I think I'm going to have to no-vote this one.

Some thoughts
-hide

Don't get me wrong, I absolutely love the fever dream imagery that you guys used here, especially in the exploration logs and in the description of the monoliths. I loved the amount of creativity that was put into this, and I really liked the idea of describing weird dream space and movement through it in terms of dimensions (4th dimension, 5th dimension, etc.). I really liked the strangely tragic ending of too. Plus, as stated by other posters, this article captured the mystery aspect of the contest super well. I can tell you, right now, that this is one of those articles whose images and story stuck with me days after I've read it.

However, maybe I'm not understanding it, but I just think that the core anomaly is logically inconsistent (yeah, I know, ironic). The article states that SCP-5000 can spread through different concepts and that any sentient being/sufficiently advanced AI can accidentally stumble upon it; which seems to be a possibility, given that "partial assimilation of sections of the Noosphere pertaining to visual abstraction, conjecture, and metacognition" has occurred, yet at the same time, it's self-contained and classified as Safe.

The first sentence of the description kind of throws me for a loop too. The more I try to analyze it, the more I find that I don't really understand what it really means in the context of what exactly the anomaly is (even though the Foundation appears to have a pretty good understanding of it).

It's also apparently "biologically self-contained within an organically inaccessible region of the Noosphere," but at the same time, the article says that the Monoliths can breach themselves into physical reality if they grow big enough, allowing nearby sentient beings to get sucked into the Noosphere.

As I understand it, even thinking of or being conceptually associated with the Anchor would put you at risk of being affected, too, as described in the line, "SCP-5000's influence is capable of spreading through physical, mental, or conceptual proximity to the Anchor".

With everything considered, it looks like the anomaly would easily be an unstoppable Keter, or would even have lead to some kind of in-progress K-class scenario, especially since some of the Monoliths include enormous clock faces at each of the Earth's poles and a giant metal sphere that's half the size of the moon.

Let me know if there's any misconceptions that I have that you guys can clear up, since I do like this article a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really cant express how much i loved this or even why really. something about the horrific dreamscape of the musicality just did it for me, and the inability to properly conceptualize a lot of this helped play into the mystery of what exactly happened. I really hope this article sees more traction as the contest goes on, even if it doesnt get the top spot

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I want some of what you guys were smoking when you wrote this.
Trippy as hell and hits the mystery vibe well, +1

item.title: Nöösphere
item.content:encoded:
I never thought I'd see a Resonance Cascade, let alone create one.

A collaboration between Bread and Bird. Giant thanks to Xilas for letting me hijack their idea, and write about violin Billy Joel and Tom Jones. I legitimately really enjoyed working on this, this was a great experience.

Thanks to TheBlueHour, not_a_seagull, Nameless Mediocre and LiterallyMechanical for critting this in its developmental stage!

Image credits are in the article info.

item.title: huzzah huzzah read the brain hurty
item.content:encoded:
My original plans for 5k were the same as my plans for 4K. That is to say, write about 6 drafts, never finish any yet constantly allude to all of them in chat. Then bread (the madlad) pmed me asking to collab. my first thought was "well shit now i have to actually settle on one draft," followed by "i thought this person had standards dammit." then we wrote this. and it was fun. very fun. read it I guess
